question.text: Scenario: Priya Sharma, aged 42, is a salaried private sector employee. Her income details for FY 2025-26 are as follows: - Basic Salary: Rs. 18,00,000 per annum - Dearness Allowance (part of retirement benefits): Rs. 3,60,000 per annum - HRA received: Rs. 7,20,000 per annum (she pays rent of Rs. 40,000 per month in Mumbai) - Employer's contribution to EPF: 12% of (Basic + DA) = Rs. 2,59,200 - Employer's contribution to NPS: 12% of (Basic + DA) = Rs. 2,59,200 - Priya's own contribution to EPF: Rs. 2,59,200 - Priya's own contribution to NPS under 80CCD(1B): Rs. 50,000 - Priya's PPF investment: Rs. 1,50,000 - Life insurance premium: Rs. 80,000 - Health insurance premium: Rs. 28,000 (self and family, all below 60) + Rs. 35,000 (parents, both senior citizens) - Interest on education loan for her son: Rs. 1,20,000 (3rd year of repayment) - Interest on self-occupied house property loan: Rs. 3,50,000 - Short-term capital gains (STT paid, listed equity): Rs. 2,50,000 - Long-term capital gains (STT paid, equity MF): Rs. 3,00,000 - Dividend income from Indian companies: Rs. 80,000 - Savings account interest: Rs. 12,000 Priya seeks advice from her investment adviser on whether to opt for old or new tax regime. Question 5 of 5: Comparing the total income tax payable (including cess, excluding capital gains which are taxed identically under both regimes), which regime should Priya choose and what is the approximate tax saving?

answer.text: Old regime is better; saves approximately Rs. 3,370 compared to the new regime
New regime is better; saves approximately Rs. 45,000 compared to the old regime
Old regime is better; saves approximately Rs. 1,25,000 compared to the new regime
Both regimes result in virtually identical tax; the difference is less than Rs. 500

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Full computation under both regimes (on normal income — STCG/LTCG taxed identically at special rates under both):
OLD REGIME: Gross Salary: Rs. 31,39,200 Less: HRA exemption u/s 10(13A): Rs. 2,64,000 Less: Standard deduction u/s 16(ia): Rs. 50,000 Net Salary Income: Rs. 28,25,200 Income from House Property (self-occupied): Interest Rs. 3,50,000 capped at Rs. 2,00,000 = (Rs. 2,00,000) Income from Other Sources: Dividend Rs. 80,000 + Savings interest Rs. 12,000 = Rs. 92,000 Gross Total Income (normal): Rs. 28,25,200 - Rs. 2,00,000 + Rs. 92,000 = Rs. 27,17,200 Less: Chapter VI-A deductions: Rs. 6,06,000 Taxable Income (normal rates): Rs. 21,11,200
Tax under old regime slabs: Up to Rs. 2,50,000: Nil Rs. 2,50,001 to Rs. 5,00,000: 5% = Rs. 12,500 Rs. 5,00,001 to Rs. 10,00,000: 20% = Rs. 1,00,000 Above Rs. 10,00,000: 30% on Rs. 11,11,200 = Rs. 3,33,360 Total tax on normal income: Rs. 4,45,860 Add cess 4%: Rs. 17,834 Total (normal): Rs. 4,63,694
NEW REGIME: Gross Salary: Rs. 31,39,200 Less: Standard deduction: Rs. 75,000 Net Salary Income: Rs. 30,64,200 Income from House Property: Nil (no interest deduction on self-occupied property allowed) Income from Other Sources: Rs. 92,000 Gross Total Income (normal): Rs. 31,56,200 Less: Section 80CCD(2): Rs. 2,59,200 Taxable Income (normal rates): Rs. 28,97,000
Tax under new regime slabs (FY 2025-26): Up to Rs. 4,00,000: Nil Rs. 4,00,001 to Rs. 8,00,000: 5% = Rs. 20,000 Rs. 8,00,001 to Rs. 12,00,000: 10% = Rs. 40,000 Rs. 12,00,001 to Rs. 16,00,000: 15% = Rs. 60,000 Rs. 16,00,001 to Rs. 20,00,000: 20% = Rs. 80,000 Rs. 20,00,001 to Rs. 24,00,000: 25% = Rs. 1,00,000 Above Rs. 24,00,000: 30% on Rs. 4,97,000 = Rs. 1,49,100 Total tax on normal income: Rs. 4,49,100 Add cess 4%: Rs. 17,964 Total (normal): Rs. 4,67,064
Difference: Rs. 4,67,064 - Rs. 4,63,694 = Rs. 3,370. Old regime is marginally better.
Capital gains tax (identical under both regimes): STCG u/s 111A at 20% = Rs. 50,000; LTCG u/s 112A at 12.5% on (Rs. 3,00,000 - Rs. 1,25,000) = Rs. 21,875. Plus cess = Rs. 2,875. Total capital gains tax = Rs. 74,750 under both regimes.
**The Trap:** Rs. 45,000 saving for new regime is a common miscalculation when candidates forget to include HRA exemption or HP interest deduction under old regime. Rs. 1,25,000 saving overstates the benefit by double-counting some deductions. The near-identical result (Rs. 500 difference) occurs if candidates make small computational errors. The correct answer shows the old regime wins by a very narrow margin of Rs. 3,370.
**Cross-Exam Context:** This marginal difference highlights why investment advisers must compute BOTH regimes for every client. The optimal regime depends heavily on the specific mix of deductions — changing even one component (e.g., higher rent, higher NPS contribution) can flip the answer.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For Priya, the old regime wins by only Rs. 3,370 — a razor-thin margin. If her employer increased NPS contribution to 14% under the new regime (which the new regime allows), the 80CCD(2) deduction would rise to Rs. 3,02,400 from Rs. 2,59,200, adding Rs. 43,200 in deductions, which would make the new regime clearly better. Advise Priya to negotiate higher employer NPS contribution if switching to the new regime.
